title: Ensure that the seccomp profile is set to docker/default in your pod definitions

input_row.title: Ensure that the seccomp profile is set to docker/default in your pod definitions
input_row.description: Enable docker/default seccomp profile in your pod definitions.
input_row.rationale: Seccomp (secure computing mode) is used to restrict the set of system calls applications can make, allowing cluster administrators greater control over the security of workloads running in the cluster. Kubernetes disables seccomp profiles by default for historical reasons. You should enable it to ensure that the workloads have restricted actions available within the container. Impact: If the docker/default seccomp profile is too restrictive for you, you would have to create/manage your own seccomp profiles.
input_row.audit: Review the pod definitions in your cluster. It should create a line as below: securityContext: seccompProfile: type: RuntimeDefault
input_row.remediation: Use security context to enable the docker/default seccomp profile in your pod definitions. An example is as below: securityContext: seccompProfile: type: RuntimeDefault Default Value: By default, seccomp profile is set to unconfined which means that no seccomp profiles are enabled. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tutorials/clusters/seccomp/ 2. https://docs.docker.com/engine/security/seccomp/
input_row.impact: If the docker/default seccomp profile is too restrictive for you, you would have to create/manage your own seccomp profiles.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** k8s_pod_security_seccomp_profile_runtime_default_enforced

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Enumerate all Pod and PodTemplateSpec resources across all namespaces using the Kubernetes API (e.g., via kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json and similar for controllers like Deployments, DaemonSets, StatefulSets, ReplicaSets, CronJobs, Jobs).
- For each Pod spec (and PodTemplateSpec in controllers), check the .spec.securityContext.seccompProfile.type field.
- Validate that the seccompProfile.type is set to RuntimeDefault (which maps to the docker/default profile as per Kubernetes documentation).
- Also check for container-level overrides: for each container in .spec.containers and .spec.initContainers, ensure .securityContext.seccompProfile.type is either unset (inherits from pod) or explicitly set to RuntimeDefault.
- Pass Criteria: Every Pod and PodTemplateSpec (and their containers, if overridden) must have seccompProfile.type set to RuntimeDefault or not set (and the pod-level is RuntimeDefault).
- Fail Criteria: Any Pod or PodTemplateSpec (or container override) where seccompProfile.type is missing, set to Unconfined, or set to a custom profile (other than RuntimeDefault).
- Example kubectl command for manual spot-checking:
  ```
  kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json | jq '.items[] | {name: .metadata.name, ns: .metadata.namespace, seccomp: .spec.securityContext.seccompProfile}'
  ```
- For controllers (Deployments, etc.), use:
  ```
  kubectl get deployment,daemonset,statefulset,job,cronjob --all-namespaces -o json | jq '.items[] | {kind: .kind, name: .metadata.name, ns: .metadata.namespace, seccomp: .spec.template.spec.securityContext.seccompProfile}'
  ```
- The automation should recursively check all relevant workload types to ensure coverage of both running pods and future pods created by controllers.

**SOURCE:** CIS KUBERNETES BENCHMARK V1.11 PDF
